title: Akkusativ
explainer_md:
The **Akkusativ** marks the direct object — the thing the action happens *to*.

Ask: **Wen oder was?** (whom or what?)

Only the masculine article changes: **der → den**, ein → einen.

| | m | f | n | pl |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominativ | der | die | das | die |
| Akkusativ | **den** | die | das | die |

*Ich sehe **den** Mann.* — I see the man.

payload.explanation: „sehen“ takes a direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„haben“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine ein → einen (der Hund).
„suchen“ takes the Akkusativ, but feminine die stays die — only masculine changes.
„essen“ takes a direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Apfel).
„trinken“ takes the Akkusativ, but neuter das stays das — only masculine changes.
„finden“ takes the Akkusativ. Neuter das Buch stays das in the Akkusativ.
„kaufen“ takes a direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Tisch).
„brauchen“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine ein → einen (der Stuhl).
„sehen“ takes the Akkusativ, but feminine die stays die — only masculine der changes to den.
„brauchen“ takes the Akkusativ, but neuter ein stays ein (das Auto) — only masculine becomes einen.
„suchen“ takes the Akkusativ. Feminine eine stays eine (die Tasche) — no change.
„suchen“ takes a direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Lehrer).
„kaufen“ takes the Akkusativ, but plural die stays die — only masculine singular changes.
„finden“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Schlüssel), even with „nicht“ in the sentence.

payload.prompt: Ich habe ___ Bruder und eine Schwester.
payload.explanation: „haben“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine ein → einen (der Bruder); feminine eine Schwester stays the same.
The object of „sehen“ is fronted, but it is still Akkusativ: der → den. The subject is „ich“.
„kaufen“ takes the Akkusativ; masculine ein → einen.
„sehen“ takes a direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„kaufen“ takes the Akkusativ, but neuter das stays das — only masculine changes.
„trinken“ takes the Akkusativ, but feminine die stays die — no change.
„brauchen“ takes the Akkusativ; masculine ein → einen.
„suchen“ takes the Akkusativ, but feminine eine stays eine — only masculine becomes einen.
„sehen“ takes a direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„finden“ takes the Akkusativ, but feminine die stays die — no change.

payload.hint: essen + Akkusativ, der Apfel — the object is fronted
payload.explanation: Even at the start of the sentence, the object of „essen“ is Akkusativ: der → den. The subject is „ich“.

payload.prompt: Sie hat ___ Hund und eine Katze. (ein)
payload.explanation: „haben“ takes the Akkusativ; masculine ein → einen. Feminine eine Katze stays the same.

payload.translation: I am buying an apple.
payload.explanation: The verb is in second position; the Akkusativ object follows.

payload.translation: We have a cat.
payload.explanation: „haben“ takes the Akkusativ; feminine eine Katze stays unchanged. The verb is in second position.

payload.translation: He is drinking the water.
payload.explanation: „trinken“ takes the Akkusativ; neuter das Wasser stays das. Subject, verb, then object.

payload.translation: She is looking for the dog.
payload.explanation: „suchen“ takes the Akkusativ: der → den (der Hund). The verb stands in second position.

payload.translation: I need a table and a chair.
payload.explanation: „brauchen“ takes the Akkusativ: both masculine objects use einen. The two objects can come in either order.

payload.translation: The man is buying the car.
payload.explanation: „kaufen“ takes the Akkusativ; neuter das Auto stays das. Der Mann is the Nominativ subject.

payload.translation: The child is eating the apple.
payload.explanation: The Akkusativ object „den Apfel“ (der → den, triggered by „essen“) is fronted; the verb stays in second position and the subject „das Kind“ follows.

payload.translation: Today I am seeing the teacher.
payload.explanation: With „Heute“ fronted, the verb stays in second position and the subject „ich“ follows it. „sehen“ takes the Akkusativ: der → den.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of „lesen“
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: „lesen“ acts on das Buch → direct object → Akkusativ. (Neuter das stays das.)

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows a dative preposition
It is the direct object of „kaufen“
It shows possession
payload.explanation: „kaufen“ acts on den Tisch → direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.

reasons: It is the subject doing the seeing
It is the direct object of „sehen“
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: Der Hund is the one who sees → subject → Nominativ, so der stays der. „Die Katze“ is the Akkusativ object of „sehen“.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows a dative preposition
It shows possession
It is the direct object of „essen“
payload.explanation: „essen“ acts on einen Apfel → direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine ein → einen.

reasons: It is the direct object of „sehen“
It is the subject of the sentence
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: „sehen“ acts on die Frau → direct object → Akkusativ. Feminine die stays die, so only the sentence role shows the case.

reasons: It is the subject because it comes first
It is the fronted direct object of „sehen“
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: Word order can change, but the article shows the case: den Mann is Akkusativ (der → den), so die Frau is the subject who sees.

title: Dativ
explainer_md:
The **Dativ** marks the indirect object — usually the person something is given to, said to, or done for.

Ask: **Wem?** (to whom?)

| | m | f | n | pl |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominativ | der | die | das | die |
| Dativ | **dem** | **der** | **dem** | **den +n** |

*Ich gebe **dem** Kind einen Apfel.* — I give the child an apple.

payload.explanation: „geben“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine: der → dem.
„zeigen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Neuter: das → dem (das Kind).
„schenken“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine: die → der (die Frau).
„bringen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine: der → dem (der Lehrer).
„geben“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine: der → dem (der Hund); das Essen is the Akkusativ object.

payload.prompt: Der Lehrer erklärt ___ Schüler das Wort.
payload.explanation: „erklären“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine: der → dem (der Schüler).
„geben“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine: die → der (die Schwester).
„zeigen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine: die → der (die Mutter).
„geben“ takes a Dativ recipient. Plural: die → den, and the noun adds -n (die Kinder → den Kindern).
„schenken“ takes a Dativ recipient. Plural: die → den (die Eltern → den Eltern; the noun already ends in -n).
„bringen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine ein → einem (der Freund).
„zeigen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine eine → einer (die Frau); den Weg is the Akkusativ object.

payload.prompt: Der Mann gibt ___ Katze die Milch.
payload.explanation: „geben“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine: die → der (die Katze).
The recipient of „geben“ is Dativ even after the pronoun „es“. Masculine: der → dem.
„zeigen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Plural: die → den, and the noun adds -n (die Männer → den Männern).

payload.prompt: Der Vater gibt ___ Sohn den Schlüssel.
payload.explanation: The recipient of „geben“ is Dativ: der → dem (der Sohn). „den Schlüssel“ is the Akkusativ object — don't mix them up.
„geben“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine: die → der.
„zeigen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine: der → dem.
„bringen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Neuter: das → dem.
„schenken“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine: die → der.

payload.prompt: Der Lehrer gibt ___ Schüler ein Buch. (der)
payload.explanation: „geben“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine: der → dem.
„erklären“ takes a Dativ recipient. Feminine: die → der.

payload.hint: zeigen + Dativ recipient, plural die Kinder
payload.explanation: „zeigen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Plural: die → den, and the noun adds -n (Kindern).
„bringen“ takes a Dativ recipient. Masculine ein → einem.

payload.hint: schenken + Dativ recipient, plural die Eltern
payload.explanation: „schenken“ takes a Dativ recipient. Plural: die → den (den Eltern).

payload.hint: geben + Dativ recipient, das Kind — the recipient is fronted
payload.explanation: Even at the start of the sentence, the recipient of „geben“ is Dativ: das → dem. „einen Apfel“ is the Akkusativ object.

payload.translation: I give the child an apple.
payload.explanation: „geben“ takes a Dativ recipient (das → dem) and an Akkusativ object (ein → einen). The Dativ noun usually comes first.

payload.translation: He shows the woman the car.
payload.explanation: „zeigen“ takes a Dativ recipient: die → der (die Frau). The neuter Akkusativ object das Auto stays das.

payload.translation: She gives the man a book (as a present).
payload.explanation: „schenken“ takes a Dativ recipient: der → dem (der Mann). „ein Buch“ is the neuter Akkusativ object.

payload.translation: We bring the teacher the bag.
payload.explanation: „bringen“ takes a Dativ recipient: die → der (die Lehrerin). Feminine Akkusativ die Tasche stays die.

payload.translation: The father gives the son the money.
payload.explanation: „geben“ takes a Dativ recipient: der → dem (der Sohn). Der Vater is the Nominativ subject, das Geld the Akkusativ object.

payload.translation: The mother explains the game to the child.
payload.explanation: „erklären“ takes a Dativ recipient: das → dem (das Kind). „das Spiel“ is the Akkusativ object.

payload.translation: I give the book to the man.
payload.explanation: The Dativ recipient „dem Mann“ (der → dem, triggered by „geben“) is fronted; the verb stays in second position and the subject „ich“ follows.

payload.translation: Today I am showing the children the school.
payload.explanation: With „Heute“ fronted, the verb stays second and „ich“ follows. „zeigen“ takes a plural Dativ recipient: die → den + -n (den Kindern).

payload.sentence: Der Lehrer gibt **dem Schüler** ein Buch.
reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of „geben“
It is the indirect object — the person who receives the book
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: The Schüler receives the book → indirect object → Dativ. Masculine: der → dem.

reasons: It is the indirect object — the person shown the way
It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of „zeigen“
It shows possession
payload.explanation: The Frau is shown the way → indirect object of „zeigen“ → Dativ. Feminine: die → der.

reasons: It is the indirect object (recipient)
It is the direct object — the thing being given
It is the subject of the sentence
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: The apple is the thing given → direct object of „geben“ → Akkusativ (ein → einen). The recipient „dem Kind“ is Dativ.

payload.sentence: **Die Mutter** schenkt dem Kind ein Buch.
reasons: It is the indirect object (recipient)
It is the direct object of „schenken“
It is the subject — the person doing the giving
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: Die Mutter is the one giving → subject → Nominativ, so die stays die. The Dativ recipient is „dem Kind“.

reasons: It is the masculine direct object of „bringen“
It is the plural indirect object — the recipients of the apples
It is the subject of the sentence
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: The Kinder receive the apples → indirect object → Dativ plural: die → den and the noun adds -n (Kindern). It only looks like masculine Akkusativ.

reasons: It is the subject because it comes first
It is the direct object of „geben“
It follows a dative preposition
It is the fronted indirect object — the recipient of the car
payload.explanation: Word order changes, but the article shows the case: dem Mann is Dativ (der → dem), the recipient of „geben“. The subject is „ich“.

title: Präpositionen + Akkusativ
explainer_md:
These prepositions ALWAYS take the Akkusativ:

**durch, für, gegen, ohne, um**

Memory aid: *do-gfou!*

*Das Geschenk ist **für den** Mann.*

title: Präpositionen + Dativ
explainer_md:
These prepositions ALWAYS take the Dativ:

**aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu**

*Ich fahre **mit dem** Zug.* — I travel by train.

title: Wechselpräpositionen
explainer_md:
Nine prepositions take **Akkusativ or Dativ**:

**an, auf, hinter, in, neben, über, unter, vor, zwischen**

- **Wohin?** (movement to) → Akkusativ: *Ich gehe **in die** Küche.*
- **Wo?** (location) → Dativ: *Ich bin **in der** Küche.*

payload.explanation: „gehen“ answers Wohin? (movement) → „in“ + Akkusativ. Feminine die stays die.
„sein“ answers Wo? (location) → „in“ + Dativ. Feminine: die → der.

payload.prompt: Er legt das Buch auf ___ Tisch.
payload.explanation: „legen“ answers Wohin? (movement) → „auf“ + Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„liegen“ answers Wo? (location) → „auf“ + Dativ. Masculine: der → dem.
„laufen“ (running to a place) answers Wohin? → „unter“ + Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„schlafen“ answers Wo? (location) → „unter“ + Dativ. Masculine: der → dem.
„gehen“ answers Wohin? (movement) → „an“ + Akkusativ. Neuter das stays das.
„stehen“ answers Wo? (location) → „an“ + Dativ. Neuter: das → dem.

payload.prompt: Sie hängt das Bild über ___ Bett.
payload.explanation: „hängen“ here is an action to a place (Wohin?) → „über“ + Akkusativ. Neuter das stays das.
Here „hängen“ describes a position (Wo?) → „über“ + Dativ. Neuter: das → dem.

payload.prompt: Ich stelle die Tasche neben ___ Stuhl.
payload.explanation: „stellen“ answers Wohin? (movement) → „neben“ + Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„stehen“ answers Wo? (location) → „neben“ + Dativ. Feminine: die → der.
„stehen“ answers Wo? (location) → „vor“ + Dativ. Neuter: das → dem.

payload.prompt: Er stellt die Lampe zwischen ___ Tisch und den Stuhl.
payload.explanation: „stellen“ answers Wohin? (movement) → „zwischen“ + Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„sich setzen“ is a movement onto the chair (Wohin?) → „auf“ + Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.

payload.prompt: Das Kind sitzt zwischen ___ Mutter und dem Vater.
payload.explanation: „sitzen“ answers Wo? (location) → „zwischen“ + Dativ. Feminine: die → der.
„gehen“ shows movement (Wohin?) → „in“ + Akkusativ. Feminine die stays die.
„sein“ shows location (Wo?) → „in“ + Dativ. Feminine: die → der.

payload.prompt: Er legt das Buch auf ___ Stuhl. (der)
payload.explanation: „legen“ shows movement (Wohin?) → „auf“ + Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„sitzen“ shows location (Wo?) → „auf“ + Dativ. Masculine: der → dem.
„fahren“ shows movement (Wohin?) → „in“ + Akkusativ. Feminine die stays die.
The picture is in position (Wo?) → „an“ + Dativ. Feminine: die → der.

payload.prompt: Sie hängt das Bild an ___ Wand. (die)
payload.explanation: She moves the picture to the wall (Wohin?) → „an“ + Akkusativ. Feminine die stays die.
„schlafen“ shows location (Wo?) → „vor“ + Dativ. Feminine: die → der.

payload.prompt: Er stellt das Auto hinter ___ Haus. (das)
payload.explanation: „stellen“ shows movement (Wohin?) → „hinter“ + Akkusativ. Neuter das stays das.

payload.prompt: Das Fahrrad steht zwischen ___ Auto und dem Haus. (das)
payload.explanation: „stehen“ shows location (Wo?) → „zwischen“ + Dativ. Neuter: das → dem.

payload.translation: I am going into the kitchen.
payload.explanation: „gehen“ shows movement (Wohin?) → „in“ + Akkusativ: in die Küche.

payload.translation: I am in the kitchen.
payload.explanation: „sein“ shows location (Wo?) → „in“ + Dativ: die → der (in der Küche).

payload.translation: The cat is sleeping under the table.
payload.explanation: „schlafen“ shows location (Wo?) → „unter“ + Dativ: der → dem (unter dem Tisch).

payload.translation: He puts the book on the table.
payload.explanation: „legen“ shows movement (Wohin?) → „auf“ + Akkusativ: der → den (auf den Tisch).

payload.translation: The car is (parked) in front of the house.
payload.explanation: „stehen“ shows location (Wo?) → „vor“ + Dativ: das → dem (vor dem Haus).

payload.translation: She puts the bag next to the window.
payload.explanation: „stellen“ shows movement (Wohin?) → „neben“ + Akkusativ. Neuter das Fenster stays das.

payload.translation: A man is waiting in front of the school.
payload.explanation: „warten“ shows location (Wo?) → „vor“ + Dativ: die → der. The fronted phrase pushes the subject after the verb.

payload.translation: The child is playing behind the house.
payload.explanation: „spielen“ shows location (Wo?) → „hinter“ + Dativ: das → dem. With the phrase fronted, the verb stays in second position.

reasons: „in“ always takes the Akkusativ
It answers Wohin? — movement into the kitchen
It is the direct object of „gehen“
It answers Wo? — a fixed location
payload.explanation: „gehen“ shows movement (Wohin?), so the two-way preposition „in“ takes the Akkusativ. Feminine die stays die.

reasons: It answers Wohin? — movement into the kitchen
„in“ always takes the Dativ
It answers Wo? — a fixed location
It is the indirect object of „sein“
payload.explanation: „sein“ shows location (Wo?), so the two-way preposition „in“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: die → der.

reasons: It answers Wo? — the book's location
It answers Wohin? — movement onto the table
It is the indirect object of „liegen“
„auf“ always takes the Dativ
payload.explanation: „liegen“ shows location (Wo?), so the two-way preposition „auf“ takes the Dativ. Masculine: der → dem.

payload.sentence: Er legt das Buch auf **den Tisch**.
reasons: It answers Wo? — a fixed location
It is the direct object of „legen“
It answers Wohin? — the book is moved onto the table
„auf“ always takes the Akkusativ
payload.explanation: „legen“ shows movement (Wohin?), so the two-way preposition „auf“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.

reasons: It answers Wohin? — movement over the table
„über“ always takes the Dativ
It is the indirect object of „hängen“
It answers Wo? — the lamp's position
payload.explanation: Here „hängen“ describes a position (Wo?), so the two-way preposition „über“ takes the Dativ. Masculine: der → dem.

payload.sentence: Sie stellt den Stuhl vor **das Fenster**.
reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It answers Wohin? — the chair is moved to the window
It answers Wo? — a fixed location
„vor“ always takes the Akkusativ
payload.explanation: „stellen“ shows movement (Wohin?), so „vor“ takes the Akkusativ. Neuter das stays das — the role, not the article, reveals the case.

title: Verben mit Dativ
explainer_md:
Some verbs take their object in the **Dativ**, not the Akkusativ:

**helfen, danken, gefallen, gehören, antworten, glauben, schmecken**

*Er hilft **der** Frau.* — He helps the woman.

title: Personalpronomen
explainer_md:
Pronouns change with case:

| Nom | Akk | Dat |
|---|---|---|
| ich | mich | mir |
| du | dich | dir |
| er | ihn | ihm |
| sie | sie | ihr |
| es | es | ihm |
| wir | uns | uns |
| ihr | euch | euch |

*Siehst du **mich**? Hilfst du **mir**?*

title: Possessivartikel
explainer_md:
**mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer** take the same endings as *ein*:

- Akkusativ m: mein → **meinen**
- Dativ m/n: mein → **meinem**
- Dativ f: mein → **meiner**

*Ich helfe **meinem** Bruder.*
